coredata.dc:title: A ratiometric fluorescent sensor based on molecularly imprinted multilevel mesoporous silica for highly sensitive detection of imidacloprid
coredata.dc:creator: Wei, Ziqi
Zhang, Weiyi
Wang, Shengjie
Han, Yiyi
Feng, Dongsheng
Ma, Yingqing
Deng, Bo
Chen, Zixin
Mao, Jialuo
Xu, Fei
Wang, Zhen
Cao, Hui
coredata.dc:description: Herein, A ratiometric fluorescent sensor based on molecularly imprinted multilevel mesoporous silica (MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs) was designed for highly sensitive detection of imidacloprid (IDP) pesticide with long half-life in soil, plants, and water environments. In this sensor, CdTe QDs were anchored onto the multilevel mesoporous of MIFP—SiCQDs by post-imprinting modification as the analytical signal to improve the detection sensitivity of the conventional doping method (d-MIFP). The results showed that the synthesized MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs possessed both small mesopore (2–3 nm) and large mesopore (5–50 nm), which is favorable for the anchoring of CdTe QDs (about 2–3 nm in diameter) and the mass transfer of IDP into them. Due to the multilevel mesoporous structure and low background, the sensor responded well to the addition of IDP in the range of 5 ng mL−1–0.5 μg mL−1 with a detection limit of 3.55 ng mL−1. The MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs sensor was successfully applied to determine IDP levels in real samples with the average recovery ranging from 97.64 to 109.88% and RSD values ranging from 1.00 to 4.65%. Furthermore, the quenching mechanism of MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs by IDP was considered to be the dynamic quenching. These results proved that the construction of multilevel mesoporous and post-imprinting modification of the analytical signal is an effective method to improve the sensitivity of d-MIP.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Imidacloprid
Detection
Ratiometric fluorescence
Post-imprinting modification
Molecularly imprinted multilevel mesoporous silica

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of SiCQDs and CdTe QDs
Quantitative analysis of IDP using MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs
The detection principle of MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs sensor
Sensitivity of MIFP—CQDs@CdTe QDs to IDP
Discussion on the mechanism of fluorescence quenching

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of SiCQDs, CdTe QDs (A), and MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs (B) synthesis.

(A) PL emission spectrum of MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs under single-wavelength excitation at 345 nm. Inset: The colour of the emission of SiCQDs and CdTe QDs under UV irradiation. (B) N2 sorption isotherms and corresponding pore size distribution obtained from the adsorption branch of MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs (inset). (C) FT-IR spectra of MIP, MIFP—SiCQDs, and MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs.
(A and B): SEM images of MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs ( × 200,000). (C and D): TEM images of MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs ( × 40,000).

(A) Fluorescence spectra of MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs with different concentrations of IDP. (Inset: PL colours under UV irradiation at λex = 365 nm). (B) Fitting curves for IDP concentrations of 5 ng mL−1–0.5 μg mL−1 for MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs. (C) Selectivity of MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs and NIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs. (D) Effect of storage time on the fluorescence stability of MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs.
Selectivity of MIFP—CQDs@CdTe QDs for IDP and analogues.

(A) Absorption spectra of CdTe QDs, IDP and CdTe QDs with IDP. (B) Absorption spectrum of IDP and fluorescence spectra of SiCQDs and CdTe QDs (λex = 340 nm, λem = 602 nm). (C) PL decay and fitted curves of MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs and MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs with IDP. (D) Signal transduction mechanism of MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs.
Comparison of different sensors for the detection of IDP in different samples.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Results for the determination of IDP in real samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: A ratiometric fluorescent sensor based on molecularly imprinted multilevel mesoporous silica for highly sensitive detection of imidacloprid

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Health Science and Engineering, Shanghai Engineering Research Center for Food Rapid Detection, University of Shanghai for Science and Technology, P.O. Box 454, No. 516, Jungong Road, Shanghai, 200093, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Health Science and Engineering
Shanghai Engineering Research Center for Food Rapid Detection
University of Shanghai for Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Health Science and Engineering, Shanghai Engineering Research Center for Food Rapid Detection, University of Shanghai for Science and Technology, P.O. Box 454, No. 516, Jungong Road, Shanghai 200093, P.R China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanghai Center of Agri-products Quality and Safety, Shanghai, 201708, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shanghai Center of Agri-products Quality and Safety

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shanghai Center of Agri-products Quality and Safety, Shanghai 201708, P.R China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, A ratiometric fluorescent sensor based on molecularly imprinted multilevel mesoporous silica (MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs) was designed for highly sensitive detection of imidacloprid (IDP) pesticide with long half-life in soil, plants, and water environments. In this sensor, CdTe QDs were anchored onto the multilevel mesoporous of MIFP—SiCQDs by post-imprinting modification as the analytical signal to improve the detection sensitivity of the conventional doping method (d-MIFP). The results showed that the synthesized MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs possessed both small mesopore (2–3 nm) and large mesopore (5–50 nm), which is favorable for the anchoring of CdTe QDs (about 2–3 nm in diameter) and the mass transfer of IDP into them. Due to the multilevel mesoporous structure and low background, the sensor responded well to the addition of IDP in the range of 5 ng mL−1–0.5 μg mL−1 with a detection limit of 3.55 ng mL−1. The MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs sensor was successfully applied to determine IDP levels in real samples with the average recovery ranging from 97.64 to 109.88% and RSD values ranging from 1.00 to 4.65%. Furthermore, the quenching mechanism of MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs by IDP was considered to be the dynamic quenching. These results proved that the construction of multilevel mesoporous and post-imprinting modification of the analytical signal is an effective method to improve the sensitivity of d-MIP.

Herein, A ratiometric fluorescent sensor based on molecularly imprinted multilevel mesoporous silica (MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs) was designed for highly sensitive detection of imidacloprid (IDP). The synthesized MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs possessed both small mesopore (2–3 nm) and large mesopore (5–50 nm), which is favorable for the anchor of CdTe QDs (about 2–3 nm in diameter) and the mass transfer of IDP into them.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Imidacloprid (IDP), a neonicotinoid pesticide, is used widely in agriculture to protect crops and food from pests. It is a selective agonist of the insect nicotinic acetylcholine receptor (nAChRs) and possesses some advantages such as efficacy, long duration, broad-spectrum, stability and high efficiency. Therefore, IDP has been widely applied globally for seed treatment (e.g., maize, sunflower, cotton) [1–3]. However, the illegal use of IDP, which is released into soil or accumulates in water and agricultural products, presents a potential hazard to the environment and human health. Several studies have concluded that IDP toxicity may have been underestimated because of its adverse impact on honey bees and bumblebees [4]. Thus, the construction of a highly selective and sensitive analytical strategy for IDP has attracted considerable research attention.

Many traditional methods have been used to detect IDP, such as gas chromatography, liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry, and high-performance liquid chromatography [5–9]. Although these methods show high sensitivity and excellent repeatability, their application is limited because of tedious and complicated sample pretreatment processes, costly instruments, and a lack of real-time monitoring. Some rapid detection methods based on sensitive elements such as antibodies and polymers have been developed for the detection of IDP. Li et al. (2018) established a highly selective method for the detection of IDP based on a poly(β-CD)-based electrochemical sensor [10]. The linear range of the proposed method was 10.20 ng mL−1–2.55 μg mL−1 with a detection limit of 4.55 ng mL−1. Fernández et al. (2019) developed a monoclonal antibody-based immunosensor for the electrochemical detection of IDP and the LOD was defined as 6.13 ng L−1 [11]. However, these electrochemical sensors may have high-cost and low environmental stability, limiting their applications in IDP detection.

Molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) have proven to be an outstanding tool in the analysis of contaminants because of their unique features of structural predictability, recognition specificity and universal application [9]. However, at present, MIPs are mainly used to adsorb targets in a mixture and are not suitable for their simultaneous detection. The construction of fluorescent sensors based on imprinted polymers is an effective way to ameliorate the above-mentioned deficiencies. These sensors have advantages of both the sensitivity of fluorescence technology and the specific recognition properties of imprinted polymers [12]. In recent years, single-wavelength imprinted fluorescent sensors with quantum dots (QDs) have been enthusiastically pursued due to their specific and rapid recognition ability in complex samples [13–15]. However, these sensors are easily affected by background interference and signal fluctuations during detection.

To circumvent the shortcomings of the above sensors, ratiometric fluorescence sensors involving the simultaneous measurement of fluorescence signals at two or more well-resolved wavelengths have been developed. Compared with the ‘single-irradiation’ mode, ratiometric fluorescence sensors with self-calibration capability can be more effective in reducing the impact of testing conditions [16–19]. Gui et al. (2018) fabricated a molecularly imprinted fluorescent polymer (MIFP) with two QDs to determine trace levels of Malachite Green [19]. In this sensor, green fluorescent CdTe QDs were encapsulated into the core of mesoporous silica microspheres to provide the reference signal and a built-in correction for environmental effects. The other red QDs were coated on the surface of the silica microspheres via the Stöber method and served as an analytical signal, thereby enhancing the precision and sensitivity of the analytical method. Another ratiometric fluorescent method was also developed on a core-shell structured molecularly imprinted sensor for the detection of trinitrotoluene and diniconazole. Importantly, this method not only achieved a lower detection limit (nM) for the targeted analytes but the visual signal output could be observed by the unaided eye [20,21].

MIFPs are generally prepared by doping QDs into the imprinted polymer matrix via the reverse microemulsion method or sol-gel method [18,22]. However, in the template elution process, QDs with smaller particle sizes are subjected to the washing solution for long durations, which decreases their fluorescence intensity and the detection sensitivity of the MIFP. Post-imprinting modification is an effective method to protect the fluorescence sensitivity and stability of the MIFP sensor. Here, MIFPs are prepared via a two-step strategy, including an imprinting polymerisation process and a subsequent modification operation by which QDs are chemically bonded to MIFP pores [12]. Using this strategy, the bonding of QDs in the pores results in their uniform distribution in the MIFPs, improving the stability of output signals.

The surface morphology and pore size of MIPs are extremely important in the post-imprinting modification process. Mesoporous MIPs are generally prepared using cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) as a template, with pore sizes of about 2–3 nm. These MIPs are unsuitable for the post-imprinting process because QDs, whose diameter is about 2–3 nm in diameter, are too large to be anchored inside the MIP pores. Additionally, the introduction of QDs in MIPs may block the mesopores and decrease the internal analyte mass transfer efficiency, thereby inhibiting the conversion from molecular recognition to the fluorescence signal output [23].

Multilevel mesoporous silica, one of the new-generation silica materials, has been fabricated to contain both small (2–3 nm) and large (>5 nm) mesopores. This ideal matrix possesses two outstanding characteristics. First, the 2–3 nm pores can improve the internal analyte mass transfer efficiency. Second, the large pores (>5 nm) provide sufficient space for the anchoring of QDs to ensure that their introduction does not decrease the adsorption capacity of the material [24]. But, there do not appear to be reports of ratiometric fluorescence sensors based on multilevel mesoporous silica for the visual recognition and detection of IDP.

In this work, multilevel mesoporous MIFPs were synthesized by the co-solvent method using IDP as the template, APTES as the monomer, TEOS as the cross-linker, carbon quantum dots (SiCQDs) as the reference signal and CdTe QDs as the analytical signal. The prepared MIFPs possessed two distinct emission peaks at 430 and 602 nm under a single excitation wavelength (340 nm), corresponding to the blue fluorescence of SiCQD and the red fluorescence of the CdTe QDs. The multilevel mesoporous properties and morphology of the MIFPs were characterised by SEM, TEM and FT-IR. Furthermore, the fluorescence quenching mechanism of the MIFPs toward IDP was determined by spectral analysis. Finally, the applicability of the proposed method was evaluated through the detection of IDP in real samples.

All chemical reagents in this experiment were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Imidacloprid (98%) was obtained from Shanghai Pesticide Research Institute Co., Ltd. Ammonia water (25%–28%), ethanol (99.5%) and ethyl ether (99.5%) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Shanghai Co., Ltd. Tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS, 99.9%), N-Acetyl-l-Cysteine (NAC, 99%), sodium tellurite (Na2TeO3 99.9%), zinc chloride (ZnCl2, 99%), cadmium chloride (CdCl2, 99.9%), cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB, 99%), 3-aminopropyl triethoxy silane (APTES, 99%), sodium citrate (98%), sodium borohydride (NaBH4, 98%), and N-Hydroxysuccinimide (NHS, 98%) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). N-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-N′-ethyl carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC, 98%) was obtained from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. Water used in the experiments was ultrapure deionised water (resistance 18 MΩ cm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of SiCQDs and CdTe QDs
ce:section.ce:para: The SiCQDs were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal method [25]. First, trisodium citrate (1.0 g, 4 mmol) as the carbon source was dissolved in deionised water (16.0 mL) in a round-bottomed flask and degassed with nitrogen for 15 min. Then, APTES (3.0 mL, 12 mmol) was injected into the nitrogen-saturated precursor solution and stirred for 5 min. The mixture was sealed in a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 200°С for 2 h for the carbonisation of trisodium citrate and in situ surface functionalisation of APTES. Subsequently, the mixture was dialysed for 24 h using a dialysis bag with a molecular cut-off of 1 kDa. The SiCQDs (Quantum yield, 20%) were collected and stored at 4°С.

CdTe QDs were prepared by a one-step hydrothermal route [26]. Here, CdCl2 (46 mg, 0.25 mmol), ZnCl2 (34 mg, 0.25 mmol) and NAC (147 mg, 0.9 mmol) were dissolved in deionised water (40 mL). The pH of the solution was adjusted to 8.0 with aqueous NaOH solution (4.0 M). Then, NaBH4 (5 mg, 0.13 mmol) and Na2TeO3 (11 mg, 0.05 mmol) were added to the mixture with vigorous stirring. After 5 min, the mixture was placed into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 48 min. Stable water-compatible CdTe QDs (Quantum yield, 59.9%) with PL emission wavelength at 602 nm were obtained and used in the present experiments.

MIFP—SiCQDs were synthesized by a co-solvent method [25]. Typically, CTAB (0.5 g, 1.37 mmol) was dissolved in an emulsion system composed of H2O (68 mL), SiCQDs (2.0 mL, 5 mg mL−1), aqueous ammonia (0.8 mL) and ethyl ether (20 mL), followed by vigorously stirring for 0.5 h. Then, a mixture of TEOS (2.5 mL), ethanol (10 mL), APTES (55 μL, 0.24 mmol) and IDP (30 mg, 0.12 mmol) was quickly dripped into the above mixture. The reaction system was vigorously stirred in the dark at 25 °C for 4 h to obtain a white precipitate. Template IDP and CTAB were washed several times by a mixture solvent of ethanol/acetic acid (8:2, v/v) until no IDP was detected by the UV–vis spectrophotometer. The non-imprinted fluorescent polymer-bonded SiCQDs (NIFP—SiCQDs) were prepared via a similar process but without IDP.

CdTe QDs were anchored to the pores of MIFP—SiCQDs via an amide reaction between the carboxylate groups of CdTe QDs and the amino groups of MIFP—SiCQDs. Briefly, CdTe QDs (1 mL) were activated at 25°С for 15 min with EDC (2 μg mL−1, 1 mL) and NHS (2 μg mL−1, 1 mL). Then, MIFP—SiCQDs (100 mg) were dispersed in water (4 mL) and added to the above solution. The resulting mixture was stirred in the dark for 4 h followed by centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 5 min, washing with ultrapure water 3–4 times and drying under vacuum. Finally, MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs (Quantum yield, 32.2%) was dispersed in ethanol (20 mL).

Photoluminescence spectra of MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs were obtained on a Shimadzu RF-6000 spectrofluorometer (Shimadzu Co., Kyoto, Japan) equipped with an automatic instrument verification function. FT-IR spectra of MIP, MIFP—SiCQDs and MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs were recorded with a Thermo Fisher Nicolet iS5 FT-IR spectrometer. UV–vis absorption spectra of CdTe QDs and IDP were obtained with a Shimadzu UV-2600i spectrometer (Shimadzu Co., Kyoto, Japan). SEM images of the MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs were recorded using an extreme-resolution analytical field emission scanning electron microscope (Tescan Mira 3 XH, operating at 20 kV) for morphological evaluation. TEM images of MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs were obtained with an Oxford Instrument X-ray spectrometer (Aztec X-MaxN 80, operating at 200 kV). Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms of MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs were recorded using an ASAP2460 (Micromeritics Instrument Corp., USA). The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method was used to calculate the specific surface area of MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs. The quantum yield (QY) of MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs was determined by the comparative method [27] (Equation.1).
(1)
Φ F = Φ F
(
S t d
)
F ·
A
S t d
·
n 2
F
S t d
· A ·
n
S t d
2
Here, ΦF(Std) is the QY of quinine sulphate standard (54.6%) [27], F and FStd are the fluorescence areas in the sample and the quinine sulphate standard; AStd and A are the absorbances in the sample and the quinine sulphate standard; n and nStd are the refraction indexes in the sample and the quinine sulphate standard.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative analysis of IDP using MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs
ce:section.ce:para: All fluorescence intensity detection was carried out under the following conditions.

The appropriate MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs solution (5 mg·L–1, 0.4 mL) was added to a colorimetric dish, and the given IDP concentration was added to the above solution at final concentrations ranging from 5 ng·mL–1 to 0.5 μg·mL–1, and a final volume of 0.8 mL. Then, the mixture was incubated at 25°С for 15 min. The fluorescence spectra were recorded under the following conditions: excitation and emission slit widths, 10 nm; excitation wavelength, 340 nm; emission range, 400–650 nm. The detection limit was calculated according to 3σ/K, where σ represents the standard deviation of 10 blank measurements and K is the slope of the calibration curve [28].

The adsorption behaviour of MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs and NIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs was compared. 50 mg of MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs or NIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs was added into an ethanol solution of IDP (100 μg·mL−1, 10 mL) and the mixture was allowed to incubate at 27 °C for 30 min. Under the adsorption conditions, the adsorption capacity (μg·mg–1) of MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs and NIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs was calculated according to Equation (1).
(1)
W =
(
C 1
−
C 2
)
V
m

The imprinting factor (IF) was calculated according to Equation (2).
(2)
I F =
W
M I F P
W
N I F P
Here, C1 (μg·mL−1) is the initial concentration of IDP in the adsorption solution, C2 (μg·mL−1) is the concentration of IDP in the supernatant after adsorption. V (mL) is the volume of the absorption solution, m (mg) is the mass of MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs, and WMIFP and WNIFP (μg·mg−1) are the adsorption capacities of MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs and NIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The detection principle of MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 A and B schematically illustrate the preparation and application process of the QDs and MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs. In this work, SiCQDs and CdTe QDs in MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QD were used as reference and response signals, respectively. SiCQDs and CdTe QDs were synthesized via the hydrothermal method [25,26]. Then, the blue SiCQDs were doped into the multilevel mesoporous molecularly imprinted polymer (MIFP—SiCQDs) as a reference signal via the co-solvent method [29]. In this process, large mesopores were formed by heterogeneous gasification of ethyl ether and small mesopores were attributed to the elution of CTAB micelles. The red CdTe QDs were further anchored on the large pores of MIFP—SiCQDs to provide the response signal via amidation of the carboxylic groups of CdTe QDs and the amino groups of MIFP—SiCQDs. Based on the fluorescence emission intensity ratio, Δ(F602/F430), a standard linear plot was constructed for the quantification analysis of IDP.

The colour and fluorescence spectra of the synthesized SiCQDs and CdTe QDs was observed using a 2F-20D blank-box UV analyser. Fig. 2 A (inset) shows that the SiCQDs (blue) and CdTe QDs (red) displayed markedly different colours under a 365 nm UV lamp, which could be readily observed with the unaided eye. The fluorescence spectra of MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs were also recorded to analyse the energy transfer behaviour of the SiCQDs and CdTe QDs. As shown in Fig. 2A, there was no overlap between the SiCQDs and CdTe QDs emission spectra. Additionally, the MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs possesses a 20 nm Stokes shift (Fig. S1). These results suggested that no energy transfer occurred in the MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs, which was beneficial for the fluorescence measurement. Notably, under a single excitation wavelength of 340 nm, the MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs exhibited two well-resolved emission peaks at 430 nm and 602 nm, indicating that the SiCQDs and CdTe QDs were successfully incorporated into MIFP.

The morphology and structure of MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs were characterised by SEM and TEM (Fig. 3 ). As shown in Fig. 3A and B, MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs exhibited a uniform spherical structure with an average diameter of about 450 nm. From the SEM images, it also be clearly observed that MIFP—CQDs@CdTe QDs possessed a multilevel mesoporous structure on different length scales from small mesopore (2–3 nm) to large mesopore (5–50 nm). Fig. 2C and D showed the TEM images of MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe. The worm-like channels, rough surfaces, and contrast of dark and pale parts in the MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe indicated the co-existence of large radially oriented mesopores and small mesopores on the surface of nanosphere. These multi-scales mesoporous structures mainly derived from the elution of the CTAB micelles and the gasification of ethyl ether. In the preparation of MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs, the hydrolysis/condensation of TEOS and self-assembly of the molecularly imprinted polymers occurred simultaneously on the surface of ethyl ether nanodroplets (ethyl ether−H2O−CTAB−NH4OH). Due to the exothermic hydrolysis of TEOS and the volatility of ethyl ether (boiling point 34 °C), ethyl ether concurrently vapourised and escaped from the emulsion system, passing through the condensed silica shell to form multilevel mesoporous silica [29]. The EDS spectrum in Fig. S2 showed the presence of C, N, O, S, Cd, Si, and Te elements which are the main elements in the MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs composite. According to this test, the synthesized MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs possess silica and CdTe QDs, as we expected.

The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm (Fig. 2B) of MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs was consistent with a typical type-Ⅳ isotherm. This isotherm contained hysteresis loops, illustrating the presence of mesopores [29]. Compared with traditional mesoporous silica, the average pore diameter of MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs was significantly increased to around 4.4 nm, which facilitated the grafting of CdTe QDs into the multilevel mesoporous silica [30,31]. The specific surface area of MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs was 476.960 m2 g−1, calculated via the Barrett-Joyner-Halenda method, and the pore volume was 0.408 cm³·g−1.

The FT-IR spectra of MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs, MIFP—SiCQDs and MIP (without bonded SiCQDs) are shown in Fig. 2C. The FT-IR spectrum of MIP exhibited a peak at 3425 cm−1; this corresponded to H-O-H stretching vibrations due to the formation of a hydrogen bond between the surface silanol groups and the remaining water molecules. The peak at 1628 cm−1 was attributed to N-H bending vibrations of the primary amines of APTES. The peaks at 1111, 950 and 796 cm−1 were assigned to Si-O-Si transverse asymmetric stretching, Si-O stretching and Si-O-Si bending vibrations, respectively, indicating the presence of a dense silica network [32,33]. Compared with MIP, the peak of MIFP—SiCQDs at 3260 cm−1 was blue-shifted by 165 cm−1, which was related to the –NH moieties of the primary amines in SiCQDs. The far-infrared peak at 548 cm−1 was associated with the vibrations of the –CO–NH– moiety [34], indicating that SiCQDs were successfully bonded by MIP to form MIFP—SiCQDs. Compared to the peak of COO− in CdTe QDs [35], the peak of MIFP—SiCQDs at 1448 cm−1 was red-shifted by 48 cm−1, revealing that CdTe QDs were anchored into the MIFP—SiCQDs via the amidation reaction. These results further confirmed that CdTe QDs and SiCQDs were successfully bonded to multilevel mesoporous silica.

The adsorption capacity of MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs and NIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs for IDP was also explored to assess their recognition ability. As shown in Fig. 4 C, the adsorption capacity of MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs and NIFP—CQDs@CdTe QDs for IDP was 5.9 and 3.7 μg mg−1, respectively, indicating that the specific recognition sites for IDP were formed in the MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs.

Fluorescence stability is also one of the important indicators of the performance of ratiometric fluorescence sensors. To assess the stability of MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs, the fluorescent intensity ratio, Δ(F602/F430), was measured from 0 to 17 d. As shown in Fig. 4D, the photoluminescence intensity of MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs gradually decreased by 27% from 0 to 8 d, which may have been due to bleaching decay through the surface and deep trapping routes, dark exciton states and nonradiative relaxation [36]. The photoluminescence intensity then remained constant from 8 to 17 d, indicating that MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs had excellent fluorescent stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity of MIFP—CQDs@CdTe QDs to IDP
ce:section.ce:para:
The sensing performance of MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs towards IDP was evaluated. As shown in Fig. 4A, as the concentration of IDP increased, the PL intensity of the CdTe QDs gradually decreased while the SiCQDs fluorescence remained essentially constant. If the fluorescence intensity ratio was closely related to the addition concentration of IDP, the relationship could be described by Equation (4):
Δ(F602/F430) = (F602/F430)0/(F602/F430)IDP
Here, (F602/F430)0 and (F602/F430)IDP are the PL intensity ratios in the absence and presence of IDP, respectively.

Based on Δ(F602/F430), a standard linear plot was constructed for the quantification analysis of IDP. Fig. 4B shows the linear fitting curve, with R2 > 0.99 reflecting an excellent linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio and the concentration of IDP. The linear equation of Δ(F602/F430) = 2.4418 + 9.7634X (R2 = 0.9959) was suitable for the quantification of trace IDP (5 ng mL−1–0.5 μg mL−1). And the detection limit was 3.55 ng mL−1. Therefore, CdTe QDs were anchored inside the MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs, which was suitable for the conversion from molecular recognition to fluorescence signal output. Furthermore, the sensitivity of MIFP—CQDs@CdTe QDs for IDP detection has been compared with other methods (Table 1 ). The MIFP—CQDs@CdTe QDs possessed higher sensitivity than piperidine-calix [4] arene modified gold nanoparticles [43], core-shell magnetic molecularly imprinted polymers [45], molecularly imprinted polymer on graphene surface [47], capacitive sensor based on molecularly imprinted polymers [48], and Zr(Ⅳ) metal-organic framework-based sensing platform [37] for IDP detection, but the sensitivity of MIFP—CQDs@CdTe QDs was inferior to that of quantum-dot-based lateral flow immunoassay for IDP detection. Although the sensitivity of MIFP—CQDs@CdTe QDs was not the highest, the determination procedure for IDP detection was much simpler and faster than the immunoassay methods. In summary, the MIFP—CQDs@CdTe QDs was desirable for the detection of IDP in environmental and agricultural samples.

To evaluate the selectivity of MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs toward IDP, different pesticides have been investigated, such as thiamethoxam, thiacloprid, acetamiprid, permethrin, and fenpropathrin. As displayed in Fig. 5 , compared with the control group, IDP has a significant effect on the fluorescence intensity of MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs. In contrast, permethrin and fenpropathrin did not cause fluorescent changes of MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs. Thiamethoxam, thiacloprid, and acetamiprid have a weak effect on the fluorescence intensity of MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs. It is because of the similarity in chemical structures with IDP, small amounts of the above pesticides are adsorbed into the imprinted cavities to lead to the slight fluorescence quenching of MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs. These results showed that the synthesized MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs has an excellent selectivity and can meet the requirement for detecting IDP.

Additionally, the effects of some potentially interfering ions—such as Co (II), Na (Ⅰ), K (Ⅰ), Mg (Ⅱ), Cd (Ⅱ), and Cu (Ⅱ)—on the performance of MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs were investigated [19]. As shown in Fig. 5, compared with the control group, there were no obvious changes in the fluorescence intensity ratio after the addition of these metal ions. It is reasonable to suppose that the IDP imprinted sites were not matched to the metal ions in size, shape, or coordinated functional groups. Hence, metal ions do not affect the performance of MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs.

The practicality of MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs was studied using river water (Huangpu River, China) and corn (Shanghai, China) samples. The river sample was filtered using a 0.22 μm membrane and the corn sample was purchased from the local market and crushed. Then, the two samples were spiked with IDP at concentrations of 0, 50, and 500 ng mL−1. As shown in Table 2 , the MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs exhibited a high recovery for IDP ranging from 91.60% to 109.88%, indicating that multilevel mesoporous structures provided sufficient space to ensure sensitivity of MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs towards IDP in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Discussion on the mechanism of fluorescence quenching
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence quenching mechanism of IDP towards MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs was further investigated in this study. According to previous reports, quenching mechanisms include dynamic quenching, static quenching, photoinduced electron transfer, the inner filter effect, Förster resonance energy transfer [38]. As shown in Fig. 6 A, there were no obvious changes in the absorption spectra of the CdTe QDs before and after binding IDP. This meant that the quenching mechanism was not due to the filtering effect [39]. Fig. 6B shows that there was no spectral overlap between the fluorescence emission spectra of MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs at 430 or 602 nm and the absorption spectrum of IDP, suggesting that the energy transfer mechanism could also be excluded.

Generally, when the quenching mechanism is static or dynamic, the ratio of unquenched fluorescence intensity versus quenched fluorescence intensity conforms to the following linear Stern-Volmer equation [36].
(5)
Δ
(
I 602
/
I 430
)
=
F 0
F
= 1 + K
[ Q ]
Here, F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity in the absence and presence of quencher, respectively, K is the quenching constant and [Q] is the concentration of the quencher.

As shown in Fig. 4B, the quenching of MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs followed a linear Stern−Volmer plot (R2 = 0.99), indicating that the quenching process was dynamic or static. To further distinguish between these two quenching mechanisms, the fluorescence lifetime of the MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs in the presence and absence of IDP was investigated.

As shown in Fig. 6C, the fluorescence lifetime of MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs was 33.57 ns after the binding of IDP, which was shorter than that of MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs (35.62 ns). According to previous reports [40,41], if the fluorescence quenching mechanism was static quenching, the lifetime of the QDs had no change in the presence of a quencher. Therefore, the main quenching mechanism of MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs with IDP may be dynamic quenching rather than static quenching. The UV–vis absorption spectra of the IDP, CdTe QDs, and IDP/CdTe QDs complexes are were investigated in this study. As shown in Fig. 6A, no ground-state complex was generated between IDP and CdTe QDs, further confirmed that the quenching mechanism of MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs by IDP was dynamic quenching. It was proposed that the electron transfer would occur between quencher and CdTe QDs during the dynamic quenching process [42]. Therefore, the quenching mechanism of MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs toward IDP can be speculated by the molecular orbital theory. Under UV excitation at 340 nm, an excited-state complex formed when IDP bound into the functional groups in the imprinted cavity. Thus, the energy of CdTe QDs would be transferred to the excited-state complex, resulting in fluorescence quenching [43,44].

In this work, a ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on multilevel mesoporous silica was successfully developed for the specific identification and sensitive detection of IDP. The combination of large mesopore (5–50 nm) and small mesopore (2–3 nm) can not only facilitate the anchor of CdTe QDs into them but also provide the mass transfer path of IDP into them. Thus, this method achieved the satisfactory quantification of IDP in the range of 5 ng mL−1–0.5 μg mL−1 with a detection limit of 3.55 ng mL−1. The overall accuracy was validated and revealed a good recovery of 97.64–109.88% for IDP. Structural analogues of IDP did not cause fluorescence quenching of MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs. In conclusion, the novel MIFP—SiCQDs@CdTe QDs sensor, which integrated multilevel mesoporous MIP and ratiometric fluorescence, provided an excellent strategy for IDP determination.

Ziqi Wei: Investigation, Data curation, Validation, Writing – original draft. Weiyi Zhang: Investigation, Methodology, Data curation. Shengjie Wang: Data curation, Editing. Yiyi Han: Methodology. Dongsheng Feng: Visualization. Yingqing Ma: Formal analysis. Bo Deng: Investigation. Zixin Chen: Software. Jialuo Mao: Resources. Fei Xu: Formal analysis. Zhen Wang: Supervision. Hui Cao: Conceptualization, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The project was sponsored by Shanghai Research Support Program (19391902200).

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2022.110775.
